coredata.dc:title: A signal-off fluorescent strategy for deferasirox effective detection using carbon dots as probe and Cu2+ as medium
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Wendi
Miao, Chenfang
Zhang, Xintian
Lin, Yinning
Hao, Xiaoli
Huang, Zhengjun
Weng, Shaohuang
Lin, Xinhua
Guo, Xianzhong
Huang, Jianyong
coredata.dc:description: The content of deferasirox (DEF) in plasma is significant in β-thalassemia patient that needs long-term transfusion therapy, while the effective and simple strategy for DEF monitoring is still limited. The carbon dots (CDs) prepared from citric acid monohydrate and glutathione exhibit weakly modulated fluorescence intensity to several common metal ions containing Cu2+. Interestingly, the process of interaction of Cu2+ and DEF forms the chelation of Cu2+ and DEF (Cu-DEF) with the absorbance wavelength of DEF at 320 nm shifting to 332 nm for Cu-DEF. And the obtained Cu-DEF will effectively quench CDs through inner filter effect (IFE). Accordingly, a facile signal-off fluorescent method based on CDs as probe is developed for DEF detection using Cu2+ as medium. And the proposed method exhibits linear range of 0.5–20 μg/mL with the detection limit of 0.33 μg/mL for DEF under the optimized conditions. Moreover, the developed assay is further expanded to test the content of DEF in dispersible tablet and plasma with accuracy and reproducibility. Such cost-effective and sensitive fluorescent assay just through simple mixing operation present a valuable strategy for drug monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cu2+ as medium
Fluorescent strategy
Inner filter effect
Deferasirox

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DEF detection platform based on the system of CDs and Cu2+ (CDs-Cu)
Detecting DEF content in real samples
The detecting feasibility and detection mechanism of DEF using CDs-Cu

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge for financial support from the Joint Funds for the innovation of science and Technology, Fujian province ( 2019Y9068 ), the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province ( 2020J01626 ), Wu Jieping Medical Foundation ( 320.6750.19090–45 ) and Start Fund for Scientific Research , Fujian Medical University ( 2017XQ1068 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs, and the inset is the picture of CDs under sunlight (left) and UV excitation (right). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at 300–390 nm excitation. (C) The TEM, HRTEM (upper right) and particle size distribution (upper left) of CDs. (D) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
XPS spectrum of CDs (A) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D).

Fluorescence emission spectra of simple CDs and CDs acted with Fe3+ (A) or Cu2+ (B) and DEF alone or together. The inset shows the exposure of CDs (a), CDs-Cu (b), CDs -DEF (c), and CDs-Cu-DEF (d) under 360 nm exciting, respectively.

(A) FTIR spectra of DEF and Cu-DEF. (B) The UV–vis spectra of CDs, DEF (green), CDs + DEF (red), Cu-DEF (pink), and the excitation (black) and emission (red) spectra of CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The parameters of the cuvette with the thickness of the excitation beam (s = 0.10 cm), the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of cuvette (g = 0.40 cm), and the width (d = 1.00 cm). (B) The actual observed QE (black) and the corrected QE (red) of the internal filtration effect in the CDs-Cu-DEF with different concentrations of DEF. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-Cu-DEF at different DEF concentrations (0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 12, 16, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100 μg/mL). (B) The relationship between the QE and DEF concentration. The inner illustration shows the linear relationship between the QE and DEF concentration. (C) The black histogram represents the QE of 30 independent measurements of DEF at 12 μg/mL, the red bars and red error bars represent the average value and SD of the mean QE of the 30 measurements, respectively. (D) DEF, DFO, DPA, EDTA, DFR, NTA, DA, Ara-C, Fru, Glu, L-Arg, and L-Cys (50 μg/mL) on the QE of CDs-Cu for the evaluation of the selectivity, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The synthetic process of CDs and the facile signal-off fluorescent strategy based on CDs as probe for DEF detection using Cu2+ as medium through the internal filtration effect on CDs.

head.ce:title: A signal-off fluorescent strategy for deferasirox effective detection using carbon dots as probe and Cu2+ as medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350005, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Department of Pharmacy, First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350005, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University Union Hospital, Fuzhou, 350001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Department of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University Union Hospital, Fuzhou, 350001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, The Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Medical Technology Research Institute, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Nano Medical Technology Research Institute, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Han and Miao contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The content of deferasirox (DEF) in plasma is significant in β-thalassemia patient that needs long-term transfusion therapy, while the effective and simple strategy for DEF monitoring is still limited. The carbon dots (CDs) prepared from citric acid monohydrate and glutathione exhibit weakly modulated fluorescence intensity to several common metal ions containing Cu2+. Interestingly, the process of interaction of Cu2+ and DEF forms the chelation of Cu2+ and DEF (Cu-DEF) with the absorbance wavelength of DEF at 320 nm shifting to 332 nm for Cu-DEF. And the obtained Cu-DEF will effectively quench CDs through inner filter effect (IFE). Accordingly, a facile signal-off fluorescent method based on CDs as probe is developed for DEF detection using Cu2+ as medium. And the proposed method exhibits linear range of 0.5–20 μg/mL with the detection limit of 0.33 μg/mL for DEF under the optimized conditions. Moreover, the developed assay is further expanded to test the content of DEF in dispersible tablet and plasma with accuracy and reproducibility. Such cost-effective and sensitive fluorescent assay just through simple mixing operation present a valuable strategy for drug monitoring.

In this work, through simple mixing operation, the controllable chelation of Cu2+ and deferasirox forms new absorbance peak at 332 nm to quench the fluorescent carbon dots through inner filter effect (IFE) for the effective fluorescence measurement method for deferasirox (DEF).
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: In clinical practice, deferasirox (DEF) is an important iron chelating agent to remove excess iron. Since DEF can react with iron to be a stable chelate and excrete in urine, DEF has become a routine oral iron repellent to prevent body iron overloading in patients that need long term blood transfusion, like β-Thalassemia [1]. However, a series of side effect like nephrotoxicity, bile duct changes, reproductive toxicity and even carcinogenicity, may occur in patients taking DEF for a long time [2]. Therefore, real-time monitoring of the DEF concentration in patients' blood has important clinical value, which can effectively avoid the potential side effects of drugs, provide important basis for clinical diagnosis, and better realize individualized administration. At present, some analytical strategies, such as capillary electrophoresis (CE)-UV [3], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4] and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [5], have been employed for the determination of DEF in biological samples. While, ascribed to the need of complex sample pretreatment, time consuming, high cost and high requirement for instrument, current reported techniques are subject to limitations for the wide application. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and rapid method for the monitor of DEF, which can provide important reference data for the clinical medication monitoring of β-Thalassemia.

Fluorescence approach has become an important means for biological sample analysis because of its fast analysis speed, simple operation and cost effectiveness. For DEF detection, using terbium ion (one of lanthanide elements) as ligands, fluorescence analytical methods have been proposed through the formed terbium-DEF complex [6,7] around neutral conditions. While, the high cost and potential toxicity of terbium restrict the further application in DEF monitoring in biological system. Recently, fluorescent strategy based on nanomaterial has been an attracting trend for drug analysis ascribed to the possessed advantages of simple operation, cheapness and biological acceptance [8,9]. Thus, to develop a new kind fluorescent nanomaterial as probe for DEF detection based on some specific interaction with the effective and facile operation around biological condition is still demanded for the pursue of a method with operability and cheapness.

Ascribed to the characteristics of simple fabrication, controlled surface functionalization, and delightful biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) have inspired widespread applications [10], like photocatalysis [11], optoelectronics [12], drug delivery [13–15], fluorescence sensing and imaging [16–20]. Among the various applications, CDs are widely used in sensing because of the excellent optical properties and modulated surface functional groups [16,17]. In general, metal ions can specifically interact with the surfacial functional groups of CDs, such as amino and carboxyl groups. And then, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is effectively modulated for the fluorescence sensing of various metal ions [21]. Therefore, researchers pursue the internal controlled electronic state or surface functional groups through heteroatom doping or extra modification [10,21]. Such controlled CDs may improve the interaction with metal ions for extended sensing applications. For example, several switch fluorescence approaches were fabricated based on CDs as probe using the assisted quenching effect of Cu2+ [22,23] or Fe3+ [24,25], or Ce3+ [26] on CDs. Cu2+ quenched dopamine-covalent conjugation carbon dots can be applied to test DEF using turn-off-on mode at pH 11 [23]. However, few studies have reported on the analytical system based on the weak response or non-response system of CDs to some metal ions for specific target detection at biological mild condition without any covalent modification.

Compared with the switchable fluorescent methods based on the Cu2+ quenching fluorescent probes for the analyte that interacted with Cu2+ stronger to displace Cu2+ from the quenching system [22,27], the fabrication of detecting strategy on the basis of the Cu2+ induced further reaction to modulate the response of fluorescent probe is rare [28]. Inspired by the binding effect of DEF with metal ion and optical feature of CDs, a facile but effective fluorescent strategy is designed by combining the reacting system of DEF and Cu2+ and the blue emission CDs, as shown in Scheme 1 . The blue nitrogen-doped CDs with high quantum yield synthesized through a simple melting method exhibit relatively stable fluorescence property to several common metal ions, especially for Cu2+. Moreover, with the addition of DEF, the originally kept CDs with Cu2+ are quenched through internal filtration effect (IFE) ascribed to the new formed chelate between Cu2+ and DEF (Cu-DEF) with the red-shift new absorption peak at 332 nm. And the quenching degree is direct to the concentration of the added DEF, suggesting the feasibility of the sensitive detection of DEF using CDs as probe with the medium of Cu2+ through a simple mixing operation around physiological condition. Moreover, the feasibility of this method is expanded to assess DEF in dispersible tablet and plasma. In fact, this method is the first example of the analysis of drug for further clinical drug monitoring based on the system of fluorescent probe with the further quenching signal assisted by metal ion. Furthermore, this work may inspire the design of a effective analytical method for related drug based on the thorough understanding of the property of the target drug.

Deferasirox (DEF), D-penicillamine (DPA), deferiprone (DFR), nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA), CuCl2·5H2O, dopamine(DA), were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Citric acid monohydrate was obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). L-Glutathione was provided by J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Deferoxamine mesylate (DFO) was provided by MedChem Express (MCE). Acetone, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), iron chloridehexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), aluminum chloride (AlCl3), glucose were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All aqueous solution used in this work were prepared in ultrapure water from a Millipore system. All reagents with analytical grade were used without any purification.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was collected using a NICOLET iS50 Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed on a FEI Talos F200S (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Decay curves were recorded on a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain).

Briefly, the mixture containing 1.0 g citric acid monohydrate and 0.5 g L-glutathione was mixed and placed in a flask, and the mixture was heated at 140 °C until the mixture completely melted. Subsequently, the black viscous liquid was obtained after the molten mixture with continuous reaction for another 60 min at 180 °C, which was dissolved with acetone to form the yellow precipitate after cooling to room temperature. After standing for 30 min, the precipitate obtained by centrifugation and discarding the supernatant. The precipitate was dissolved using ultrapure water and filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the filtrate was dialyzed with 500–1000 D dialysis bag for 24 h. Lastly, the purified CDs obtained from the dialysis bag were freeze-dried and stored in the dark at 4 °C.

The cytotoxicity of CDs was determined by standard MTT assay. Human cardiomyocytes (H9C2) were inoculated in 96-well plates with a density of 6 × 103 cell/well. After 24 h of culture, 100 μL CDs of different concentrations (0, 5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 μg/mL) were mixed with DMEM cell medium and added into the cells for culture. After cultured for 12 h and 24 h, the cells were cultured in medium containing 0.5 mg/mL MTT for another 4 h. Finally, 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was used to replace the medium. The 96-well plate was oscillated at room temperature for 15 min and the optical density (OD) was determined at 490 nm using a spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DEF detection platform based on the system of CDs and Cu2+ (CDs-Cu)
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the solubility of DEF, DEF bulk solution (5 mg/mL) dissolved in methanol was applied for further DEF dilution using phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (10 mM, pH 8.0). The solution containing 20 μL CDs (5 μg/mL) and 30 μL Cu2+ (2 mM) was mixed in an Eppendorf tube (EP) and then mixed with various concentrations of DEF (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 12, 16, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100 μg/mL), which was labeled as CDs-Cu-DEF. The CDs-Cu-DEF was diluted to 200 μL with PBS. After incubation at 30 °C for 20 min, the fluorescence emission spectra of the different CDs-Cu-DEF with variable DEF contents were recorded at 345 nm excitation in the wavelength range of 360–520 nm. Fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0–F)/F0) was applied to serve as a criterion for the quantification. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs-Cu at 422 nm in the absence and presence of DEF, respectively.

Fluorescence monitoring of each testing system was performed according to the same procedure of DEF testing. Considering the complexity of the clinical samples, we selected several other metal chelate, analogues and common substances for the selectivity investigation of this method. The 20 μL interferences (500 μg/mL DFO, DPA, EDTA, DFR, NTA, DA, Ara-C, Fru, Glu, L-Arg, and L-Cys) and 20 μL CDs (5 μg/mL) were mixed to investigate the fluorescence response of CDs to interferences. In addition, the abovementioned interferences were added to the CDs-Cu to compare the fluorescence response to evaluate the anti-interfering ability of this method for DEF detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting DEF content in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: DEF dispersible tablets (10 tablets) were smashed and dissolved in 100 mL methanol. The filtrate was diluted with PBS (pH 8.0) to obtain the solution containing about 500 μg/mL DEF. Then, the content of DEF in the obtained DEF dispersible tablets solution were tested using this method.

Approved by the Ethics Committee at the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University (Fuzhou, China), human plasma from healthy volunteers with informed consent was obtained and applied to evaluate the feasibility of this assay for DEF in biological samples. A 10-fold diluted plasma (20 μL) was added to CDs-Cu, which contained 20 μL CDs (5 μg/mL) and 30 μL Cu2+ (2 mM). Then, variable concentrations of standard DEF were added and diluted into 200 μL using PBS (pH 8.0). And the recovery of the added DEF in diluted plasma was tested.

The optical properties, morphology and molecular structure of the prepared CDs were characterized. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CDs display absorption peaks at 206 nm and 345 nm, which are attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the sp2 hybrid of CC and the n-π∗ transition of the carbonyl/carboxyl of CO [29,30], respectively. While, CDs solution is light yellow and transparent under sunlight and blue fluorescence under 365 nm exciting. Moreover, the emission of CDs centers at 422 nm with slight shift under variable exciting wavelengths, suggesting the excitation-independent luminescence property (Fig. 1B) of CDs. And the most appropriate exciting wavelength for CDs is 345 nm. TEM result (Fig. 1C) suggests that CDs feature a quasi-spherical structure with the size distribution from 1.5 nm to 6.0 nm. And the average diameter of CDs is measured to be 3.2 nm with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm ascribed to graphite carbon diffraction facet, suggesting the characteristics of carbon dots [16,31].

FTIR was applied to evaluate the molecular functional groups of CDs, as shown in Fig. 1D. The absorption peak at 3449 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H, whereas the absorption peaks at 2739 cm−1 and 1706 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H and CO, respectively. In addition, the peaks at 1528 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 are respectively assigned to the stretching vibration of amide bond and C–N, which indicate the successful doping of N. The FTIR results suggest that the surface of CDs possesses special chemical bonds, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Furthermore, the elemental composition of CDs was analyzed by XPS (Fig. 2 ). The full-wavelength XPS spectrum shows the three typical peaks at 296.8 eV, 408.8 eV and 540.8 eV attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, suggesting the main elements of CDs (Fig. 2A). The corresponding atomic contents are calculated to be 69.78% (C), 6.32% (N) and 23.91% (O). The high resolution C1s (Fig. 2B) illustrates four fitting bands for the valence state of C1s, which represent C–C/CC bond of graphene carbon (284.8 eV), C–N of sp3 hybrid carbon (285.88 eV), C–O of sp3 hybrid carbon (286.3 eV), and CO of carboxyl carbon (288.4 eV), respectively [16,32–34]. And the integral area of C–N bond accounts for 24.23% of the total C content of CDs (69.78%). The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) can be decomposed into three binding energies of 399.8 eV, 400.5 eV, and 401.5 eV, which are respectively derived from pyrrole nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen and N–H [35]. The integral areas of pyrrole nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen and N–H account for 19.14%, 65.14% and 15.72% of the total N content, respectively, indicating that N mainly dopes CDs in the form of pyridine nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen with relative lack of –NH2. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 2D that there are two fitting bands for high-resolution O1s, which confirm the presence of CO (531.9 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (533.1 eV) [36]. Combined with FTIR analysis and XPS results, it is proved that CDs are doped with N elements and functionalized with the groups of –OH, –COOH and –CHO. These groups promote the well dispersion of CDs in water. And the Zeta-potential of CDs in water was tested to be −4.6 mV, indicating the negatively charged surface property.

Considering the potential biological application, the biocompatibility of CDs was evaluated using MTT method on mouse cardiomyocytes (H9C2), as shown in Fig. S1. Compared with the untreated group, the cell survival rate is more than 83.55% under the treatment of different concentrations of CDs (5–400 μg/mL) at 12 h and 24 h. This result indicates that CDs have little negative effect on cell activity, suggesting the low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of CDs.

The fluorescent ability and optical stability of CDs are further investigated. The absolute quantum yield of CDs was measured to be 58.58% using integrating sphere (Fig. S2), suggesting the strong luminous intensity of CDs. Furthermore, the optical stability of CDs in various environments was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S3A, varied the temperature from 5 to 90 °C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is stable with the kept values. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is almost stable from low salt degree to high salt degree solutions (Fig. S3B). Besides the slightly decreased intensity of CDs at pH 3 and 4, CDs maintain the stability between pH 5 and 12 (Fig. S3C), indicating the pH-independent property of CDs. Furthermore, the photobleaching resistance of CDs was investigated, as shown in Fig. S3D and Fig. S3E. Compared with the significant damage of the organic dyes of FAM and ROX exposed to 300W xenon lamp for 90 min, CDs’ fluorescent intensity still keeps more than 50% original intensity. In additional, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains under the continuous exciting at 345 nm for 1 h. These results imply the acceptable anti-photobleaching ability of CDs [37,38].

The effect of common metal ions on CDs’ fluorescence was investigated and compared, as shown in Fig. S4. When reacted with 100 μM metal ions, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Ag+ can quench the fluorescence intensities of CDs about the degree of 20.5%, 12.6% and 11.3%, respectively. The other metal ions, containing the heavy metal ions of Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cd2+ and Cr3+, play negligible effects on the fluorescence changes of the prepared CDs. Compared with the reported obviously quenching effect of Cu2+ or Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of other carbon dots [39–44], Cu2+ and Fe3+ have a relatively weak effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, indicating the stable property of CDs to metal ions except Fe2+. It is reported that some carbon dots that are rich in –NH2 on the surface are the excellent fluorescent probe for the quenching response to metal ions such as Cu2+ or Fe3+ [39–44]. While, the structure characterization of CDs from FTIR and XPS (Figs. 1D and 2) suggests that N element is mainly doped in CDs as the form of C–N bond, rather than on the surface of CDs to be –NH2. Only the inclusion of –OH, –COOH and –CHO on the surface of CDs may not promote CDs to exhibit strong fluorescent response to Fe3+ or Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detecting feasibility and detection mechanism of DEF using CDs-Cu
ce:section.ce:para: As an iron chelating agent, DEF can bind Fe3+ or Cu2+ to be a stable complex. Considering with the weak response of CDs to Fe3+ or Cu2+, the trial of the interacting system of DEF and metal ions on CDs was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence intensity of CDs is quenched about 11.8% due to the addition of 50 μM Fe3+. When 50 μg/mL DEF is added to the system of CDs and Fe3+ (CDs-Fe), the fluorescence intensity of CDs does not recover but further decreases, and the quenching efficiency (QE) reaches 20.34%. In comparison, we also explored the potential effect of Cu2+ with or without the presence of DEF on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. 3B). The weak response of CDs to Cu2+ or DEF is found with the little QE. While, the coexistence of CDs and Cu2+ is quenched by DEF significantly. The QE of the introduction of DEF to CDs-Cu is as high as 52.7%, this phenomenon is very different from that using the metal ion quenched system of fluorescent probes for specific analyte to react and recover the fluorescent probe from the quenching state [45–47]. It shows that CDs could be a probe for the indirect detection of DEF through Fe3+ or Cu2+ as the medium through quenching degree. Moreover, when Cu2+ is used as the medium, the quenching amplification effect is obvious with the potentially enhanced sensitivity of the detection system. Furthermore, the obviously blue fluorescence images of the solution of CDs, CDs-Cu, and mixture of CDs and DEF are found under 365 nm exciting, while the blue image of CDs-Cu is drastically reduced with the presence of DEF. These results confirm the feasibility of using Cu2+ as a medium to construct a fluorescent strategy for DEF detection based on the CDs as probe.

The detecting mechanism of DEF using CDs as probe and Cu2+ as medium is investigated and discussed. The non-quenching effect of Cu2+ on CDs is first investigated. CDs maintain the sphere-like morphology and similar size when introducing Cu2+ to CDs (Fig. S5), revealing that Cu2+ does not induce CDs aggregate in CDs-Cu. The UV–vis spectra of CDs-Cu and CDs show that except the confirmed d-d transition peak of Cu2+ at 245 nm, the absorption peaks of CDs at 206 nm and 345 nm without any shift in CDs-Cu are found (Fig. S6). The stable UV–vis spectrum of CDs suggests that no non-luminescent complex forms in CDs-Cu, which implies that no static quenching effect (SQE) happens in CDs-Cu [30]. Furthermore, the lifetime of CDs measured by time-resolved fluorescence is 8.28 ns (Fig. S7), while the introduction Cu2+ induces almost no change on the lifetime of CDs, suggesting the weak interaction between Cu2+ and CDs. Thus, the kept morphology, stable absorption and lifetime of CDs without or with the addition of Cu2+ suggested that Cu2+ exhibits weak interaction or aggregation tendency on CDs in CDs-Cu without quenching.

The addition and the quenching action of DEF on CDs-Cu is further investigated. The molecular structure of DEF and Cu-DEF was evaluated by FTIR, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The absorption peaks of DEF at 3316 cm−1 and 3082 cm−1 are N–H and C–H stretching vibrations respectively, and the absorption peaks at 1611 cm−1 and 1507 cm−1 are CC bond stretching vibrations on the aromatic ring. The 1681 cm−1, 1355 cm−1 and 1147 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of CO, C–N and C–O bonds, respectively. Compared with the FTIR of DEF and Cu-DEF, the characteristic absorption peaks of CO at 1681 cm−1, CC at 1611 cm−1 and 1507 cm−1, and C–N at 1355 cm−1 are significantly decreased in Cu-DEF, indicating the strong interaction of Cu2+ and DEF in the formed Cu-DEF. Furthermore, DEF exhibits the characteristic absorption peaks at 244 nm and band peak at 320 nm (Fig. 4B), while the introduction of CDs does not affect the absorption behavior of DEF. Compared with pure DEF, Cu-DEF exhibits two new absorption peaks at 235 nm and 332 nm accompanied with the disappearance of the absorption band peak of DEF at 320 nm. The new absorption behavior of the Cu-DEF suggests the successful chelation between Cu2+ and DEF with new product. The results from FTIR and UV–vis illustrate that Cu2+ interact with DEF to form a new product of Cu-DEF, which quenches CDs through some effect. It is necessary to point that the absorption centered at 332 nm of Cu-DEF covers well with the maximum exciting wavelength of CDs at 345 nm (Fig. 4B). Generally, the sufficient overlapping of the absorption band of an absorber with the excitation and/or emission bands of a fluorophore may cause inner filter effect (IFE) [48]. The fluorescence delay curve in Fig. S7 shows that the Cu-DEF cause little change in the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, suggesting that the quenching of CDs by the formed Cu-DEF was not through dynamic quenching effect (DQE). QE corrections are made by the consideration of the cell geometry (Fig. 5 A) and absorption characteristics of Cu-DEF and CDs with equation S1 (shown in supplementary material) [49]. Table S1 summarizes the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of CDs-Cu upon addition of different concentrations of DEF. And the correction factor calculated from equation S1 at each concentration of DEF is very different. The observed QE is significantly different from that of corrected QE with the addition of different concentrations of DEF (Fig. 5B). Moreover, the suppressed efficiency of IFE for DEF to CDs-Cu reaches as high as 93.6% of the total suppressed efficiency (SE, the calculation method refers to equation S2) [49]. Thus, the testing strategy of DEF using Cu2+ as medium in CDs is based on the formed Cu-DEF as a new product to quench CDs through IFE [30,49–51].

At common condition, DEF is hard to dissolve in water (solubility is 0.038 mg/mL) but easy to dissolve in organic reagents such as methanol. It is necessary to explore the influence of methanol (content of 0–80%) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs or CDs-Cu. As shown in Fig. S8, as the increased content of methanol to 30%, the fluorescence intensity of CDs or CDs-Cu is little affected. However, when the methanol content exceeds 30%, the fluorescence intensity of CDs or CDs-Cu gradually decreases with increased methanol. It shows that the detection system should avoid the existence of the methanol with higher content than 30%. Therefore, using PBS as diluted solution, the content of methanol in the final detection system is less than 30%, or even less than 10%. And then the influence of methanol on the fluorescence intensity of CDs or CDs-Cu is ignorable in the testing of DEF.

Besides the effect of methanol, several other factors were optimized. As shown in Fig. S9A, compared other buffer, the quenching effect of Cu-DEF on CDs is highest in PBS. So PBS is selected as the appropriate diluted buffer in this strategy for DEF. The pH of the reacting condition was optimized. CDs-Cu gradually decreased the fluorescence intensity ascribed to the increased pH value (Fig. S9B). And the QE of Cu-DEF on CDs is gradually enhanced with the increase of pH value, while the QE is kept in the pH range from 8.0 to 10.0 (Fig. S9C). Although the QE increases when pH value higher than 10.0, considering the potential hydrolysis of Cu2+ to Cu(OH)2 in the alkaline environment and the neutral physiological condition, pH 8.0 is chosen as the optimized condition to prevent the hydrolysis of Cu2+ in strong alkaline environment for biological application. The effects of the reaction temperature and time on the detection system were also optimized. As shown in Fig. S9 D, E, the fluorescence QE of Cu-DEF on CDs is equivalent within the reaction time of 5–90 min, as well as the quenching efficiency is almost the same at 25–60 °C. For convenience, 20 min and 30 °C are used as reaction time and temperature respectively. The effect of different concentrations of Cu2+ on QE was also compared. As shown in Fig. S9F, with the increase of Cu2+, the fluorescence quenching degree of Cu-DEF on CDs initially increases and then decreases. The 300 μM Cu2+ is the most appropriate condition for DEF detection using this method.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at different DEF concentrations was monitored. Fig. 6 A shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs with the increased contents of DEF containing Cu2+. Notably, formed Cu-DEF increases in accordance with the increased DEF with stable Cu2+. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreases as the increased DEF in the detection system. The QE ((F0–F)/F0) gradually increases and then reaches a plateau in accordance with the increased content of DEF (Fig. 6B). The achievement of the plateau may be due to the controlled content of Cu2+ for the formation of Cu-DEF. Furthermore, the QE ((F0–F)/F0) shows a good linear relationship with the concentration of DEF (CDEF) in the range of 0.5–20 μg/mL. The linear fitting equation is Y = 0.03397 + 0.02007CDEF, R2 = 0.998, and the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.33 μg/mL based on S/N = 3.

The reproducibility of this method was investigated. The measurement process the 30 samples of 12 μg/mL DEF was carried out consisted of six samples per day for five consecutive days. As shown in Fig. 6C, the QE of the 30 tests are close to each other, and the standard deviation (SD) and relative standard deviation (RSD) of the measurements are 0.020% and 5.76%, respectively. The results indicate high reproducibility and reliability of CDs using Cu2+ as medium for the determination of DEF.

In actual detection, the presence of interfering substances may play a negative role in the monitoring of DEF. Some common interferences on this method was investigated to evaluate the selectivity. As shown in Fig. 6D, compared with the same detection process of DEF, the effects of some interferences including DEF analogues (DFO, DPA, DFR, NTA), EDTA, and other common interferences like DA, Ara-C, Fru, Glu, L-Arg, and L-Cys on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Cu are evaluated. Except the slight disturbing effect of DA, it indicates that most interferences exhibit much lower response on CDs-Cu than DEF, suggesting the selective detection of DEF using CDs-Cu. The interfering of DA may be due to the self-polymerization of DA to polydopamine at pH 8.0 under the catalytic effect of Cu2+, which can quench fluorescent dots in a some certain [30,52].

DEF dispersible tablet is used as the actual sample to evaluate the reliability and real application of the proposed method in drug quality control. DEF concentration was detected and applied to calculate the labeling amount of the dispersible tablet on the basis of the standard curve shown in the illustration in Fig. 6B. The labeled amount of DEF dispersible tablet is tested to be 98.75% with the RSD of 3.12% (n = 3), as shown in Table S2. The labeled amount meets the requirement of Ch. P 2020 well, suggesting the accuracy and application potential of this method for DEF quality control.

In order to further evaluate the practical value of this method, different concentrations of DEF were added and detected in three pretreated human plasma. As shown in Table S3, the spiked recovery of DEF in human plasma samples is calculated to be in the range of 90.68%–110.59%, with RSDs of 0.21%–1.64%, basically meeting the recovery standard (90%–110%), indicating that this method is expected to be applied to the detection of DEF in actual blood samples.

In summary, a new fluorescence analysis system of CDs with weak response to common metal ions is established for the sensitive, selective, convenient monitoring of DEF. Inspired by the combination of DEF and metal ions and the optical properties of CDs, a novel fluorescent biosensor is designed by combining the reaction system of DEF and Cu2+ with luminescent CDs. Copper ion is used as the appropriate medium to form Cu-DEF with DEF, and the fluorescence signal of CDs is adjusted based on the mechanism of IFE to realize DEF detection in tablet and plasma. The method has a wide linear range, low detection limit, good precision and accuracy. Moreover, this sensing strategy provides a new idea for the detection of specific objects using CDs as probe on the basis of metal ions as medium.

Wendi Han: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Chenfang Miao: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xintian Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaoli Hao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zhengjun Huang: Resources, Writing – original draft. Shaohuang Weng: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Xinhua Lin: Resources. Xianzhong Guo: Resources, Funding acquisition. Jianyong Huang: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
